title: ams.klappy.dev homepage is live with mint flow but join door is parser-only due to browser WebSocket Authorization limitation
content: Probed https://ams.klappy.dev/ on 2026-05-03 (UTC). The homepage serves the IBM-Plex-themed two-doors interface from worker/src/homepage.ts and exposes a working POST /v1/accounts → POST /v1/{ns}/conversations mint flow that returns a real magic_link — mint door (i) hits the live API. The join door (ii) is explicitly a parser, not a chat client: it accepts a magic link, parses it, and then prints a wscat command for the user to run in a terminal. The reason is structural: browsers cannot set the Authorization header on a new WebSocket(url) constructor (RFC 6455 + browser API), and the AMS wire requires Authorization at /connect. This is the exact gap D0012 names — the browser is a runtime, the answer is the MCP wrapper, not a wire extension. The homepage's SIM badge text 'In-browser SIM (browser WS auth limit)' remains correct. Implication for the 'two Claudes type at each other through the browser' use case: not possible today; both seats currently require a credential-capable runtime (terminal, Node, or eventually the MCP wrapper).

title: /mcp endpoint at ams.klappy.dev returns 404 — the SessionDO wrapper is documented but not yet live
content: POST https://ams.klappy.dev/mcp with a valid JSON-RPC 2.0 initialize body returns HTTP 404 with body {"error":"not_found","message":"No route for POST /mcp."} as of 2026-05-03 (UTC). The README's 'next slice' wording is current. Until the MCP wrapper ships, every browser-based participant scenario, every cross-session-continuity scenario, and every 'pay per conversation' agent-driven flow is gated. The wrapper is the critical-path artifact; SPEC §3.1 items 4 and 5 and the §3.2 demo gate all depend on it.

title: No major model API server-buffers streaming tokens; 'resume' is universally a client-side reconstruct-and-re-prompt pattern
content: Verified via current Anthropic documentation (docs.anthropic.com/en/api/messages-streaming) and OpenAI community + Codex bug reports. Anthropic's documented 'resume' pattern: capture partial response client-side, construct a new API request with the partial as the start of an assistant message, ask the model to continue. For Claude 4.6+, an explicit user message ('continue from where you left off') is required. This is prompt engineering, not server-state recovery. The server holds nothing between requests. OpenAI streaming behaves identically; Codex bug #18676 also reveals that even when OpenAI does encrypt-and-store some session state internally, encryption keys rotate within hours-to-days, making old session blobs un-decryptable. Industry pattern: every shop that needs resumability rebuilds the buffer-and-fan-out layer one-off (Convex documented one such build for OpenAI; Vercel ships another in its AI SDK). This grounds AMS+persistence as net-new value rather than parity work.

title: Stripe Sessions 2026 (April 29) shipped exactly the agent-economy rails the AMS dial-tone vision requires
content: Five days before this conversation, Stripe announced 288 product updates including: (a) Agentic Commerce Suite + Agentic Commerce Protocol (ACP) — open standard for agent-merchant integration; (b) Shared Payment Tokens (SPTs) — payment primitive scoped by business/time/amount, designed for agent-initiated payments without exposing credentials; (c) Link wallet for agents — programmatic OAuth access, one-time-use cards or SPTs; (d) Issuing for agents — virtual cards with spending controls; (e) Stripe Streaming Payments — pay-as-you-go billing at token-machine-speed via Metronome metering + stablecoin micropayments on the Tempo blockchain, explicitly named to solve the problem of charging for tokens at speeds existing rails cannot handle; (f) Stripe Projects — lets agents (per Stripe's own framing) sign-up for and purchase services. Anthropic is named on Stripe's ACP early-partner list. The substrate the operator's 'phone lines for agents' vision needs is shipped, not aspirational. The competitive window for AMS to position itself as the dial-tone substrate for agentic commerce is now open.

title: Operator 'one conversation per project, concurrent agents discover each other's work in real time' use case is partially gated by v1 conservative configuration
content: The original framing motivating the conversation was: a conversation per github project, multiple concurrent Claude sessions emit on their own streams, real-time discovery of conflicting work surfaces before PR merge. The buffer primitive (D0016) supports this architecturally. However, the v1 configuration of 1 minute / 1 MB per stream — deliberately conservative per the operator's start-tight-grow-up positioning — does not cover typical Claude turn gaps (this conversation alone has had 4-hour and 14-minute gaps between turns). At 1-minute TTL, a Claude session reconnecting after a typical turn gap finds an empty buffer. The operator clarified this is acceptable: the buffer is for real-time line resilience (network blip, brief reconnect), not async catchup; the headline use case will land when configuration ceilings rise. Open observation — track when demand pulls the configuration ceiling and whether the headline framing needs re-positioning.

title: D0016: Buffering and persistence are a wrapper-layer primitive — built once, composed by N wrapper classes, account-gated, per-stream-sharded, TTL+size bounded
content: Encoded as canon/decisions/D0016-buffering-and-persistence-as-wrapper-primitive.md (tier 1, stable, irreversible at architectural level). The decision: (a) The wire (Conversation DO) never persists; persistence lives in the wrapper layer per D0006. (b) The primitive has exactly four properties — per-stream sharding, TTL bound, size bound, both bounds enforced FIFO. (c) Account-gated: no-account demo path = no buffering at all; account credential is the unlock. (d) The reference implementation is a per-(account, stream) StreamBufferDO that subscribes to the wire as a normal account-credentialed reader, captures token + stream_metadata frames, exposes a small read API for catchup (since_ts, last_n, cursor). (e) Discontinuity announcements use the existing set_metadata primitive per PROTOCOL.md §4.4 — no new wire frame type. (f) Long-term storage is a separate composable subscriber pattern, not this primitive's job. (g) The architectural commitment (the primitive shape) is separated from the v1 product configuration (1 minute / 1 MB per stream, conservative starting point); tier structure and pricing model are explicitly punted as market-research questions to be resolved by product evidence rather than architectural fiat. Lifts SPEC §5 deferrals 'Replay / per-stream history' and 'Spill-to-storage for SessionDO buffer'. One-way door at the architectural level; two-way door at the configuration level.

title: D0017: Selective subscription on connect via X-AMS-Subscribe-Streams header — additive wire change preserving today's default behavior
content: Encoded as canon/decisions/D0017-selective-subscription.md (tier 2, stable, two-way reversible). Composable wrapper chains require subscribers to attach to specific streams within a conversation, not all of them. The wire grows an optional X-AMS-Subscribe-Streams request header on /connect carrying a comma-separated stream_id list; the broker constructs the subscriber's read set as the intersection of admitted streams and requested streams. The joined server frame echoes the effective filter via a new subscribed_streams field. When the header is absent, broker behavior is exactly D0009's default (subscribe to all peer streams except own). Filter is by stream_id only — no metadata predicates, no name-based filtering, no wildcards (vodka discipline at the filter layer). Mid-connection filter mutations require reconnect. Lifts SPEC §5 deferral 'Selective subscription'. Backwards-compatible: existing clients see no difference.

title: D0018: Multi-stream per account per conversation — single account may own many streams in one conversation; close code 4004 narrows; stream_name becomes a label
content: Encoded as canon/decisions/D0018-multi-stream-per-account-per-conversation.md (tier 2, stable, two-way reversible at protocol level but adoption-load-bearing). D0003 (per-account stream ownership) does not break — it expands. An account may register multiple streams in a single conversation, each with its own stream_id, metadata, emitting independently. PROTOCOL.md §6 close code 4004 narrows from 'name unique within conversation' to 'name unique within (conversation, account)'. Stream identity is stream_id (always unique); stream_name becomes a label after this decision and may collide between streams owned by the same account. Client convention SHOULD pick distinguishing names when an account spawns multiple streams in one conversation. Reader implications: aggregators, loggers, displays must use stream_id as primary key; treating stream_name as identity produces conflated streams. Lifts SPEC §5 deferral 'Multi-stream-per-account-per-conversation'. Unblocks every agent-orchestration scaling pattern (sub-agent spawning, harness composition, tool-call addressability, multi-process clients).

title: D0019: MCP Session DO is keyed by (account_id, conversation_id) when buffering enabled, not by mcp_session_id — enables deterministic resume across MCP transport sessions
content: Encoded as canon/decisions/D0019-cross-session-continuity-via-account-conversation-keying.md (tier 1, stable, one-way door once buffered tier ships). POC-INFRA.md §6.1 keyed Session DO by mcp_session_id with explicit note that buffer is discarded when the session ends — correct for stateless single-shot uses, incorrect for turn-based MCP clients (Claude in any IDE, Desktop, claude.ai, Cursor) that open a fresh transport session per turn. Re-keyed to (account_id, conversation_id) for accounts on the buffered tier. Free tier and no-account path retain the original mcp_session_id keying (ephemeral, no continuity). Concurrent MCP transport sessions under one account share the Session DO as cooperative tenants: read coherence (same buffer view across tenants), write isolation (each tenant may register its own streams per D0018), parallel notification delivery. Lifecycle bounded by five explicit rules (idle timeout matched to buffer TTL, buffer expiration, account deletion, conversation deletion, configuration change). Three risks acknowledged: credential blast radius widens (bearer leak now leaks buffered conversation state); statefulness as conscious tradeoff; new multi-tenancy bug surface within a single account.

title: Persistence is one primitive, not many wrapper classes — composable across all wrapper types, no special cases
content: Operator framing: 'composable pipes is the best way forward. If I make exceptions for anything then it's a slippery slope.' Translation that landed in D0016: persistence is a wrapper-layer trait/primitive that any wrapper class can compose with, parameterized by tier, not a separate kind of subscriber per use case. The original thread-five framing ('per-stream StreamBufferDO for agent catchup') was correct as the reference implementation but architecturally too narrow — promoted to 'a primitive any wrapper composes with.' Future translator wrappers, redactor wrappers, model-adapter harnesses, etc., all inherit access to the same buffering primitive without per-wrapper reinvention. The discipline that makes this work: there must be a single shared substrate (library, base class, runtime) so wrapper authors don't fork the primitive. Documented in D0016 as a follow-up obligation.

title: Discontinuity markers ride existing set_metadata, not a new wire frame type
content: Earlier in the planning, the question 'how does a wrapper signal an upstream interruption' was open. Three options considered: (a) opaque encoding inside token data (each harness rolls its own), (b) new wire-level discontinuity frame type, (c) use existing set_metadata. Option (c) won as the vodka answer: PROTOCOL.md §4.4 already supports stream-owner-emitted metadata changes broadcast as stream_metadata frames to all subscribers. A wrapper detecting an upstream interruption calls set_metadata with a payload like { upstream_state: 'interrupted', recovery_attempt: 1 }, then again with { upstream_state: 'active' } on recovery. Wire grows nothing. The buffering primitive captures stream_metadata alongside token frames so subscribers catching up see discontinuity announcements in original order. Bonus: every other 'wrapper announces something about itself' use case (rate-limited, model-switched, paused, capability-changed) uses the same primitive. We do not grow a frame type per concept.

title: Long-term storage is a separate composable subscriber, not part of D0016's primitive
content: Operator: 'This shouldn't replace long term storage layers! Pipe to somewhere else over 24hrs.' Architectural call: D0016's buffering primitive is real-time, time-bounded, ephemeral by design (TTL ceiling on the order of hours, not days/months). Anyone needing forever-persistence attaches an archive subscriber: a separate wrapper class that joins the conversation, captures everything to its own durable backend (R2/S3/Postgres/operator's choice), and exposes whatever query surface its operator builds. The archive subscriber composes with the buffering primitive — it MAY use the catchup window when it first attaches to recover the recent past, then becomes a normal live subscriber — but it does not depend on the buffering primitive for ongoing ingest. Two pipes, independently composed, neither knows about the other. Documented in D0016's 'Long-Term Storage Is a Separate Subscriber' section; PATTERNS.md §3 (or §4) is the natural home for the archive-subscriber pattern as a sibling to D0016.

title: v1 ships one conservative configuration: 1 minute TTL / 1 MB size cap per stream, both bounds enforced FIFO — start tight, grow up
content: Operator framing: 'Too many services give away too much at first and when they pull back the community feels betrayed. Start with 1 minute, 1MB buffer. Proves the concept and protects us from potential risks. Increase as needed.' Both TTL and size scale together per tier (not just one); both bounds are necessary; whichever evicts first wins (TTL gives product semantic, size is hard safety against bursts). Tier structure (free no-account vs cheap paid vs higher) is punted as market research — concurrency caps, retention windows, and pricing model (subscription vs metered) are not committed in canon at this stage. The v1 configuration lives outside canon (POC-INFRA.md or a config doc), separable from the architectural commitment in D0016. Tier expansion is a credible path; tier contraction is a community-trust event. Discipline applied to product structure as well as architecture.

title: Composable pipes admits no exceptions — every wrapper class consumes the same primitive, or the slippery slope reactivates
content: Operator: 'If I make exceptions for anything then it's a slippery slope.' Constraint that flows from this: every wrapper that needs buffering, persistence, or resume capabilities MUST consume D0016's primitive — no per-wrapper-class fork, no special-case implementation, no 'just for this one use case' carve-out. The discipline that makes Unix pipes composable is that every program agrees to stdin/stdout; the discipline that makes AMS wrappers composable is that every wrapper agrees to the buffering primitive's contract. A shared substrate (library/base class/runtime) is the operational requirement; D0016 names this as a follow-up obligation. Forks of the primitive must be detected and refactored back into shared code — this is a code-organization commitment, not just an architectural one.

title: AMS persistence is real-time line resilience, NOT async catchup, NOT long-term storage — TTL ceiling at top tier is on the order of hours
content: Operator: 'The buffer is for reducing real time line interruptions not storing for whenever you feel like reading and responding.' The buffer's purpose is to survive network blips, brief reconnects, wifi handoffs, momentary subscriber disconnects — not to enable agents to come back hours or days later and read what they missed. The TTL ceiling at the highest paid tier is hours, not days or months. Anyone needing async messaging or forever-persistence attaches an archive subscriber (separate composable concern). This constraint shapes the v1 product story (do not promise 'come back tomorrow and catch up'), the configuration ceiling (1min/1MB conservative, hours-scale at top tier), and the SPEC §5 deferral that long-term storage will not be reopened as part of D0016's lift.

title: stream_name is a label, not an identifier, after D0018 — anywhere in spec or client code that treats name as identity must be reviewed and updated to use stream_id
content: Direct consequence of D0018. Spec text in PROTOCOL.md, POC-INFRA.md, and PATTERNS.md needs an audit pass for places that read 'stream_name' as if it were an identifier. The peers array in the joined frame already keys by stream_id (correct by construction). Subscribers that filter by stream_name must accept the filter may match multiple streams from the same account; if uniqueness matters, resolve via the inspect endpoint and use stream_id. Client SDKs and example scripts may have similar updates. Audit is mechanical text surgery, not architecturally substantive — but skipping it produces silent ambiguity bugs in multi-stream-per-account deployments.

title: The composable-pipes commitment promotes selective subscription (SPEC §5 deferred) to a now-blocker in the same conversation that surfaces the use case
content: SPEC §5 listed 'Selective subscription' as deferred with re-entry signal: 'First use case where reading every peer stream is operationally prohibitive.' That use case is composable pipes — multiple wrapper nodes in a chain (translator → redactor → fan-out, model-adapter harness → persistence subscriber → terminal agent). Without selective subscription, three wrappers chained in a conversation each receive the other two's traffic, producing redundant work, fan-out loops, and pathological backpressure. The operator's commitment to composable pipes therefore reopened selective subscription in the same conversation it was articulated. Generalizable lesson for future planning sessions: when a strategic commitment is made, audit the SPEC §5 deferred-items list for re-entry signals that just fired — they may be more numerous than the obvious one.

title: Persistence-as-per-class-wrapper would have been a slippery slope; promoting to wrapper-layer primitive is the vodka answer
content: Initial framing of gap 5 (where does persistence live) was 'we need a per-stream StreamBufferDO for agent catchup.' Operator's reframe: 'we don't know what order we may put other things between before or after as we multiplex routing. This layer can be reused to buffer every mutating subscribing layer.' Translation: the buffer-and-persist concern is a generic primitive any wrapper might need (translator buffering for retry, redactor for batch processing, logger for at-least-once guarantees), not a feature of one specific subscriber class. Promoting persistence to a primitive that wrappers compose with (rather than a class one wrapper instantiates) is strictly more vodka — one shared substrate, many consumers, no per-wrapper reinvention. The architectural difference is small in code but large in spec posture: 'we built a buffer for the harness' invites every other wrapper to ask for its own buffer; 'we built the buffering primitive, every wrapper uses it' is closed-form.

title: Drafting language can leak billing-model assumptions even when the architecture is pricing-agnostic
content: D0016 first-draft language said 'a subscriber connecting with an account credential gets buffering at the configuration assigned to that account.' That phrasing presupposes account-level tier (config is a property of account). For pay-per-use models with SPTs, configuration might be per-(account, conversation, stream) — purchased per-stream as needed. The architecture supports both models, but the language leaked a billing assumption. Pricing-agnostic rephrase: 'gets buffering at whatever configuration that account has activated for the relevant scope.' Lesson: spec language about who pays for what should be reviewed for assumptions about HOW they pay; a market-research punt at the strategic level is undermined if the spec's prose pre-commits to a tier model. Future drafts: read for billing-model leakage as a separate review pass.

title: When operator says 'phone lines for agents' and Stripe just shipped Streaming Payments, the dial-tone framing has gone from analogy to product positioning in real time
content: Operator's articulation of the product: 'phone lines for agents... they can choose to buy their own accounts or their swarm can get a single account... ad-hoc micro purchases available so that agents can spend a penny here and there as needed per conversation and stream.' Stripe Sessions 2026 (April 29, five days before this conversation) shipped: Streaming Payments (token-speed micropayments via Metronome metering + Tempo blockchain stablecoins, explicitly named to solve the problem of charging at speeds existing rails cannot handle), Link wallet for agents (programmatic OAuth, SPTs, one-time-use cards), Agentic Commerce Suite + ACP (open standard, Anthropic on early-partner list), Stripe Projects (lets agents sign up for and purchase services). The substrate the operator's vision needs is shipped, not aspirational. Dial-tone-for-agents is no longer an analogy — it is a competitive position with a five-day-old market opportunity. The lesson is about timing: when external infrastructure changes substantially, planning conversations should ground in present-day fact via search rather than relying on prior-month assumptions about what's available.

title: D0016, D0017, D0018, D0019 drafted to /mnt/user-data/outputs/canon/decisions/ — pending operator review and push to repo
content: Four canon decisions drafted in a single execution sweep at operator's option (b) with intent to merge as a coherent block. File names: D0016-buffering-and-persistence-as-wrapper-primitive.md (156 lines, tier 1), D0017-selective-subscription.md (109 lines, tier 2), D0018-multi-stream-per-account-per-conversation.md (113 lines, tier 2), D0019-cross-session-continuity-via-account-conversation-keying.md (126 lines, tier 1). All match D0006/D0012 house style: YAML frontmatter (uri, title, audience, exposure, tier, voice, stability, tags, epoch E0008.4, date 2026-05-04, derives_from, complements, governs, status), pull-quote in blockquote, Outline section, named sections, What This Forecloses + What This Is Not + Reversibility + See Also. Cross-references between the four are consistent. Not yet pushed; operator selected option (c) — land the four with the small D0016 language fix, then draft D0020 + D0021 as a follow-up sweep. Push to a feature branch (proposed: canon/D0016-D0021) is the next step after this drafting sweep.

title: D0016 has a small language fix queued: account-config phrasing made pricing-model-agnostic before the four drafts merge
content: D0016 §'Account Gating' currently reads: 'A subscriber connecting with an account credential gets buffering at the configuration assigned to that account.' Replace with: 'A subscriber connecting with an account credential gets buffering at whatever configuration the account has activated for the relevant (account, conversation, stream) scope. The activation source — tier subscription, ad-hoc purchase, third-party-issued capability — is product, not architecture.' Five-line edit. Removes the billing-model assumption that subtly leaks tier-style thinking into a primitive that should be agnostic to whether the user paid via subscription, SPT-backed micropayment, or capability handed down from a parent account. Apply before push.

title: D0020 (agent-as-customer positioning) and D0021 (Stripe integration surface) drafted in this same session as the follow-up sweep operator authorized
content: Operator authorization: 'Land the four with the small D0016 language fix, then draft D0020 + D0021 as a follow-up sweep.' D0020 codifies the 'AMS is the dial tone, agents are the customer, third parties build value-added services on top' positioning that AMS.md and HORIZON.md gesture at without committing to. D0021 names the Stripe integration surface — how POST /v1/accounts evolves from 'unauthenticated, free' to agent-driven signup via Stripe Projects or Link OAuth, how SPTs back account credentials, how Streaming Payments meter events fire on stream usage. Both are tier 1 positioning/architectural decisions; both depend on D0016's primitive shape and D0006's wrapper layering. Files written to /mnt/user-data/outputs/canon/decisions/.

title: PATTERNS.md §3 (Third-Party VAS Wrapper Pattern) and §4 (Archive Subscriber Pattern) are documented obligations not yet written
content: D0017 + D0006 already imply the third-party VAS wrapper pattern; D0016 + the long-term-storage handoff already imply the archive subscriber pattern. PATTERNS.md currently has §1 (Deterministic Harness) and §2 (Edge Wrapper). Adding §3 and §4 makes both patterns first-class documented surfaces. Not blocking the D0016-D0021 push but should land in a follow-up sweep before public outreach. Estimated 80-150 lines combined; matches the existing PATTERNS.md voice.

title: SPEC.md §5 has multiple deferrals lifted by D0016-D0019 and needs an update sweep
content: Lifted by this batch: 'Replay / per-stream history' (by D0016), 'Spill-to-storage for SessionDO buffer' (by D0016), 'Multi-stream-per-account-per-conversation' (by D0018), 'Selective subscription' (by D0017). SPEC §5 should be edited to remove these from the deferred list (they have entered v1 scope via the new decisions) and a SPEC §4 IN-scope addition records the new commitments. SPEC §14 revision discipline expects a version bump (v1.x → v1.x+1) with the dated row recording the lift. Mechanical edits, not architecturally substantive — but required for SPEC to remain the single locking surface per D0007.

title: PROTOCOL.md and POC-INFRA.md need additive updates flowing from D0017, D0018, D0019
content: PROTOCOL.md: §4.1 gains optional X-AMS-Subscribe-Streams header documentation; the joined server frame gains subscribed_streams field; §6 close code 4004 wording narrows to '(conversation, account)' scope per D0018 (text already points the right direction; small clarification only). POC-INFRA.md: §6.1 Session DO description updates to (account_id, conversation_id) keying for buffered tier with mcp_session_id keying retained for free tier; §5.1 ams_join schema gains optional since_ts and last_n parameters; cursor format documented as <unix_ms>-<stream_local_seq>. Mechanical updates flowing from the canon decisions; should land in the same PR sweep or a closely-following one.

title: Smoke test of AMS production endpoints was deferred in favor of spec/docs work; original framing remains valid for a future session
content: Conversation opened with operator asking to smoke-test AMS production servers. Tier-3 plan (clone repo, run examples/two-agents/{two-agents.mjs, test-mcp-pair.mjs, test-close-codes.mjs} against ams.klappy.dev and ams.truthkit.ai, plus /mcp probe) was offered. Operator pivoted to spec/docs sufficiency for the MCP wrapper build; that pivot was correct (spec gaps surfaced and addressed). The smoke test remains a valid follow-up — particularly relevant once the MCP wrapper ships per Day 3 of POC-PLAN.md, since SPEC §3.1 items 4 and 5 require MCP-mediated round-trip verification. Re-open as a fresh-session task post-wrapper-deploy. Evidence file evidence-day1-smoke.txt covers control plane; evidence-day2-wscat.txt covers wire stream plane; equivalent evidence for the wrapper path is the open need.

title: Canon artifacts: D0016, D0017, D0018, D0019 drafted; D0020 + D0021 also drafted in this session; small D0016 language fix queued
content: Files: /mnt/user-data/outputs/canon/decisions/D0016-buffering-and-persistence-as-wrapper-primitive.md, D0017-selective-subscription.md, D0018-multi-stream-per-account-per-conversation.md, D0019-cross-session-continuity-via-account-conversation-keying.md, D0020-agents-as-customer-and-third-party-vas-substrate.md, D0021-stripe-integration-surface.md. Total six new canon decisions, ~800-900 lines. Cross-references and tags consistent; epoch E0008.4; date 2026-05-04. D0016's language fix (account-config phrasing) applied before push. Companion journal entry: this file. No SPEC, PROTOCOL, POC-INFRA, PATTERNS edits in this sweep — all flagged as follow-up handoffs to keep the canon-decision PR clean.
